content.text: Multi-head attention runs
h independent scaled dot-product attention operations in parallel

, each attending to the sequence through a different learned linear projection. The results are concatenated and projected back to the model dimension. Every modern LLM — GPT-4, Claude, Llama, Gemini — is built on stacks of this mechanism. Understanding it precisely is the clearest path to understanding why context windows are expensive, why attention sinks exist, and how agent systems scale.
What it is
Single-head attention computes one set of attention weights over the sequence. Multi-head attention instead applies
separate attention functions in parallel, each with its own projection matrices
where

. Each head sees a different low-dimensional subspace of the full token representation, learns a different notion of relevance, and produces its own context-weighted output. Those outputs are concatenated column-wise and linearly projected back to
using a learned output matrix
. The total parameter cost is the same as one large attention block — the heads share the available dimensions, they do not add to them.
Why it matters

A single attention head learns one linear relationship between queries and keys. For most sentences and code fragments, useful information is not one-dimensional: a pronoun may refer syntactically to the nearest noun phrase, yet semantically to a concept mentioned much earlier. A tool call may depend simultaneously on the system prompt's permission rules, the user's stated goal, and the function signature from a prior turn. Multiple heads let different subspaces specialise — empirical studies show heads differentiate along syntactic, positional, and semantic axes — none of which a single projection can capture at once. This is why GPT-2 (12 heads per layer) produced markedly better outputs than equivalent single-head baselines, and why head count became a primary hyperparameter in every subsequent model.
Benefits
Diverse representation subspaces: each head learns an independent projection, collectively covering syntactic, semantic, and positional patterns
Parallel computation: all h heads run simultaneously on modern hardware — no sequential dependency between heads
No parameter overhead: by splitting d_model across h heads, total weight count equals one single-head attention block
Ensemble-like robustness: some heads can be ablated without catastrophic output degradation, suggesting redundant coverage of critical patterns
Interpretability handles: individual heads can be inspected with attention maps to understand what relationship the model is tracking at each layer
Composable with positional encodings: each head's projection amplifies or suppresses positional signals independently, allowing heads to specialise by relative position range
Trade-offs
Quadratic memory and compute in sequence length: attention scores form an
matrix per head per layer, so doubling context length quadruples cost
Reduced per-head capacity: with d_model=768 and h=12, each head sees only 64 dimensions — too narrow for very complex relationship types unless compensated by depth
Attention sink phenomenon: a few tokens (especially BOS) accumulate disproportionate attention weight in early layers, wasting head capacity on unimportant positions
KV-cache memory at inference: key and value tensors for all past tokens must be stored per head per layer, growing linearly with context — the dominant memory cost in long-context deployments
Difficult to tune head count post-training: pruning heads requires fine-tuning to recover accuracy; head count is usually fixed at model creation time
How it works
Scaled dot-product attention (one head)
For a single head with projected queries
, keys
, and values
each shaped
The scaling factor
prevents the dot products from growing large and softmax from saturating as
increases. The softmax converts raw scores into a probability distribution over the sequence; multiplying by
produces a weighted combination of value vectors — a soft, differentiable lookup.
Multiple heads in parallel
For head
, compute:
Each set of projection matrices
is learned independently. Because the heads share the full input

but project it differently, they observe the same tokens from different geometric angles. Head 1 might rotate the space to align queries with subjects; head 2 might align with verbs; head 3 might track position-relative patterns.
Concatenation and output projection
The outputs of all heads are concatenated along the feature dimension, then projected back to
The concatenated tensor has shape
. The output projection
recombines information from all heads into a single representation that feeds forward to the FFN sublayer.
Causal masking in decoder-only models
For autoregressive generation, the scores
have an upper-triangular mask applied before softmax: future positions are set to
so they become zero weight after softmax. This ensures token
can only attend to tokens
. The same multi-head machinery is used during prefill (all prompt tokens processed at once) and decode (one token per step using the KV cache).
Architecture

The diagram shows the full data flow: input token embeddings are projected to Q, K, V per head, each head runs scaled dot-product attention, outputs are concatenated, then an output projection restores the d_model shape.
When to use it
Multi-head attention is built into every transformer-based LLM. The practical decision points as a practitioner are:
Training a custom model: choose head count such that
is at least 32–64 dimensions per head — smaller heads under-represent capacity
Fine-tuning: heads update together with the rest of the transformer — no special handling needed beyond standard training hygiene
Interpretability: inspect individual heads when diagnosing why a model misses a dependency or over-attends to certain tokens
Efficient inference with GQA or MQA: production deployments replace full multi-head attention with grouped-query or multi-query variants to shrink KV cache memory without significant quality loss
Building custom cross-attention (e.g. for a retrieval-augmented agent): implement multi-head attention directly to control masking, positional bias, or query source
How to implement
The following NumPy implementation computes multi-head self-attention from first principles, making the math directly visible in code without framework abstractions.

import numpy as np


def softmax(x: np.ndarray, axis: int = -1) -> np.ndarray:
    """Numerically stable softmax."""
    e = np.exp(x - x.max(axis=axis, keepdims=True))
    return e / e.sum(axis=axis, keepdims=True)


def scaled_dot_product_attention(
    Q: np.ndarray,
    K: np.ndarray,
    V: np.ndarray,
    mask: np.ndarray | None = None,
) -> tuple:
    """
    Q, K: (n, d_k)   V: (n, d_v)
    Returns (output, attention_weights).
    """
    d_k = Q.shape[-1]
    scores = Q @ K.T / np.sqrt(d_k)      # (n, n)
    if mask is not None:
        scores = np.where(mask, -1e9, scores)
    weights = softmax(scores)              # (n, n)
    return weights @ V, weights


class MultiHeadAttention:
    """Minimal NumPy multi-head self-attention."""

    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int, seed: int = 0) -> None:
        assert d_model % num_heads == 0
        self.h = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads
        rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
        scale = np.sqrt(2.0 / (d_model + self.d_k))
        # Per-head projection stacks, shape (h, d_model, d_k)
        self.W_Q = rng.normal(0, scale, (num_heads, d_model, self.d_k))
        self.W_K = rng.normal(0, scale, (num_heads, d_model, self.d_k))
        self.W_V = rng.normal(0, scale, (num_heads, d_model, self.d_k))
        # Output projection (d_model, d_model)
        self.W_O = rng.normal(0, scale, (d_model, d_model))

    def forward(self, X: np.ndarray, causal: bool = False):
        """
        X: (n, d_model)
        Returns (output, head_weights) where output is (n, d_model)
        and head_weights is a list of h arrays each (n, n).
        """
        n = X.shape[0]
        mask = None
        if causal:
            # Upper-triangular mask: position i cannot attend to j > i
            mask = np.triu(np.ones((n, n), dtype=bool), k=1)

        head_outputs = []
        head_weights = []
        for i in range(self.h):
            Q_i = X @ self.W_Q[i]  # (n, d_k)
            K_i = X @ self.W_K[i]
            V_i = X @ self.W_V[i]
            out_i, w_i = scaled_dot_product_attention(Q_i, K_i, V_i, mask)
            head_outputs.append(out_i)
            head_weights.append(w_i)

        concat = np.concatenate(head_outputs, axis=-1)  # (n, d_model)
        return concat @ self.W_O, head_weights


if __name__ == "__main__":
    n, d_model, h = 6, 64, 8
    X = np.random.randn(n, d_model)
    mha = MultiHeadAttention(d_model=d_model, num_heads=h)
    out, weights = mha.forward(X, causal=True)
    print(f"output shape : {out.shape}")           # (6, 64)
    print(f"head 0 weights: {weights[0].shape}")   # (6, 6)
    # Verify causal: upper-triangle should be near zero
    upper = weights[0][np.triu_indices(n, k=1)]
    print(f"max upper-tri weight: {upper.max():.6f}")
PyTorch implementation with fused projection
The following PyTorch implementation uses a fused QKV projection, the standard approach in production models, and demonstrates batched head processing.

import math
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F


class MultiHeadAttentionPT(nn.Module):
    """PyTorch multi-head self-attention with fused QKV projection."""

    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int, dropout: float = 0.0):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % num_heads == 0
        self.h = num_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // num_heads
        self.d_model = d_model
        # One matrix for all Q, K, V — split at forward time
        self.qkv_proj = nn.Linear(d_model, 3 * d_model, bias=False)
        self.out_proj = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.drop = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def _to_heads(self, x: torch.Tensor, T: int) -> torch.Tensor:
        """(B, T, d_model) -> (B, h, T, d_k)"""
        B = x.size(0)
        return x.view(B, T, self.h, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor, causal: bool = False):
        B, T, _ = x.shape
        qkv = self.qkv_proj(x)               # (B, T, 3*d_model)
        Q, K, V = qkv.split(self.d_model, dim=-1)
        Q = self._to_heads(Q, T)             # (B, h, T, d_k)
        K = self._to_heads(K, T)
        V = self._to_heads(V, T)

        scale = 1.0 / math.sqrt(self.d_k)
        scores = (Q @ K.transpose(-2, -1)) * scale  # (B, h, T, T)

        if causal:
            mask = torch.triu(
                torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device), diagonal=1
            ).bool()
            scores = scores.masked_fill(mask, float("-inf"))

        attn = self.drop(F.softmax(scores, dim=-1))
        out = attn @ V                        # (B, h, T, d_k)
        # Merge heads -> (B, T, d_model)
        out = out.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, self.d_model)
        return self.out_proj(out), attn


if __name__ == "__main__":
    layer = MultiHeadAttentionPT(d_model=512, num_heads=8)
    x = torch.randn(2, 16, 512)
    out, attn = layer(x, causal=True)
    print(out.shape)   # torch.Size([2, 16, 512])
    print(attn.shape)  # torch.Size([2, 8, 16, 16])
Examples

The Hugging Face Transformers library exposes attention weights per head for inspection — directly useful when diagnosing why an agent model misses a dependency or over-attends to injection artifacts.

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch

model_id = "bert-base-uncased"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained(model_id, output_attentions=True)
model.eval()

sentence = "The agent called the weather tool because the user asked."
inputs = tokenizer(sentence, return_tensors="pt")

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)

# outputs.attentions: one (1, num_heads, seq_len, seq_len) per layer
layer_0 = outputs.attentions[0]
print(f"Layers      : {len(outputs.attentions)}")
print(f"Shape layer0: {layer_0.shape}")  # (1, 12, seq_len, seq_len)

tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(inputs["input_ids"][0])
agent_idx = tokens.index("agent")
head_4_row = layer_0[0, 4, agent_idx]   # attention from 'agent' in head 4
top3 = head_4_row.argsort(descending=True)[:3]
print("Top-3 tokens attended to from 'agent' (head 4, layer 0):")
for rank, idx in enumerate(top3):
    print(f"  {rank+1}. '{tokens[idx]}'  weight={head_4_row[idx]:.3f}")
Real-world example
Llama-3-8B uses d_model=4096, 32 attention layers, 32 query heads, and 8 KV heads (GQA). The per-head dimension is

. At inference with a 16k-token context, the attention score matrix per query head is 16,000 × 16,000 = 256M entries per layer. Flash Attention tiles this computation across SRAM so the full matrix is never materialised in HBM. The KV cache for one request at 16k tokens costs approximately 32 layers × 2 KV matrices × 8 KV heads × 128 dims × 16,000 tokens × 2 bytes ≈ 4.2 GB — the dominant memory cost for long-context agent loops.

For GPT-3 (d_model=12,288, 96 layers, 96 heads): each head has d_k=128. At the training sequence length of 2,048 tokens, the attention compute per layer is approximately 2 × 96 × 2,048² ≈ 807M multiply-adds for attention alone — with the linear projections an order of magnitude larger. This is why training GPT-3 required thousands of A100 days.
Common mistakes
Forgetting the
scaling: unscaled dot products saturate softmax into near-one-hot distributions, preventing diffuse attention and causing vanishing gradients
Applying the causal mask after softmax rather than before: future positions still receive small but non-zero weight, violating autoregressive semantics
Missing contiguous() before view: after transposing (B, h, T, d_k), calling view() without contiguous() raises RuntimeError — use .contiguous().view() or .reshape()
Choosing h such that d_k is not a power of two: many CUDA kernels are optimised for power-of-two d_k values; odd sizes silently fall to slower code paths

Transposing the wrong dimensions when splitting heads: the sequence (B, T, d_model) -> (B, h, T, d_k) requires a specific transpose and view order; a wrong transpose produces correct-looking shapes but incorrect attention patterns
Masking all positions in a row: softmax(-inf, ..., -inf) = NaN — always ensure at least one valid (unmasked) position per query row to prevent NaN propagation
Best practices

Use Flash Attention in production: torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention (PyTorch 2.0+) dispatches automatically to Flash Attention 2 on CUDA, providing 3–8x speedup and O(n) HBM memory instead of O(n²)
Fuse QKV projections: one Linear(d_model, 3*d_model) with a split is faster than three separate Linear(d_model, d_model) calls due to reduced memory bandwidth
Set bias=False on projection matrices: biases in Q, K, V have negligible quality impact and add parameters that complicate weight tying
Apply dropout to attention weights (after softmax, before the matmul with V) at 0.1 during training to prevent head collapse toward degenerate patterns
For cross-attention with a fixed encoder, pre-compute and cache K and V projections once per source sequence — this eliminates redundant projection compute during autoregressive decode

Diagnose heads by visualising attention maps: uniform rows indicate dead heads, spike to one token indicates an attention sink, all-near-zero rows indicate rank collapse — each signals a distinct training or implementation issue
Performance & scalability
Variant
KV heads
KV cache size
Used in
Multi-Head Attention (MHA)
Original Transformer, GPT-2, BERT
Multi-Query Attention (MQA)
Gemini 1.0, PaLM
Grouped-Query Attention (GQA)
Llama 3, Mistral, Qwen
Flash Attention 2 (exact MHA)
O(n) — no n×n matrix in HBM
All modern training runs

KV cache is the memory bottleneck at long-context inference: a 32-layer model with 8 KV heads, d_k=128, float16, at 16k tokens costs approximately 32 × 2 × 8 × 128 × 16,000 × 2 bytes ≈ 2.1 GB per request
GQA with 8 KV heads out of 32 query heads reduces KV cache by 4× with negligible quality loss on most benchmarks — the standard choice for new open-source models
Sliding window attention (Mistral) and ring attention (research) break quadratic scaling by restricting attention span, enabling sub-quadratic long-context models
Tooling & ecosystem
torch.nn.MultiheadAttention
: PyTorch built-in, supports batch_first=True, key_padding_mask, and attn_mask — good for research prototypes
torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention
(PyTorch 2.0+): dispatches to Flash Attention, xFormers, or math fallback — the recommended choice for all new code
flash-attn
(Tri Dao et al.): standalone package for Flash Attention 2 and 3; required for context lengths beyond 8k at competitive training throughput on A100/H100
xFormers
: Meta's library of efficient attention ops; integrates with Hugging Face Transformers via the attn_implementation config flag
BerViz
: interactive visualisation of per-head attention patterns in Jupyter notebooks — invaluable for interpretability work
TransformerLens
(Neel Nanda): mechanistic interpretability library with activation hooks into every attention head; used for studying head specialisation in GPT-2 and other open models
Comparison with alternatives
Mechanism
Context capture
Strengths
Weaknesses
Multi-Head Attention
Parallel full-context weighted sum
Universal, parallelisable, interpretable
Quadratic cost; large KV cache
Linear Attention
Kernel trick approximation
O(n) compute and memory
Lower quality on long-range dependencies
LSTM / GRU
Recurrent hidden state
O(n) memory; no KV cache needed
Sequential; poor very-long-range recall
State Space Models (Mamba)
Selective scan over state vector
O(n) inference; competitive quality
Fewer interpretability tools; newer ecosystem
Sparse Attention (Longformer)
Local + global token attention
Near-linear cost at long context
Custom CUDA kernels; harder to deploy
Security & reliability

Prompt injection exploits attention: a malicious instruction buried in a long context can attract high attention weight if it mimics system-prompt style — structural delimiters and careful positional ordering of instructions matter for agent security

Lost-in-the-middle effect: LLMs consistently under-attend to content in the middle of long contexts relative to beginning and end — critical agent constraints (tool schemas, safety rules) should not be placed mid-prompt

Attention rollout and gradient-based saliency are useful red-team tools: they reveal which input tokens the model weighted most for a given output, helping identify attention-based vulnerabilities in pipelines
NaN propagation through fully-masked rows: softmax(-inf, ..., -inf) = NaN — always ensure at least one valid unmasked position per query row; never mask all KV positions
KV cache isolation in multi-tenant inference: per-request KV cache isolation must be enforced in shared infrastructure — cross-contamination would expose one user's context to another
Key takeaways
Multi-head attention runs h independent attention operations in parallel, each in a d_k-dimensional subspace; outputs are concatenated and re-projected to d_model
Total parameter count equals a single full-dimensional attention:
— heads add diversity, not parameters
Scaling by 1/sqrt(d_k) prevents softmax saturation and vanishing gradients — this is not optional
Causal masking (upper-triangular -inf before softmax) enables autoregressive generation without changing the core computation
Quadratic cost in sequence length is the dominant scaling constraint for long-context agents; GQA and Flash Attention are the main production mitigations
Individual heads are interpretable and can diagnose model behaviour, prompt placement effectiveness, and attention-based security vulnerabilities
Mental model

Think of each attention head as a specialist analyst in a committee. Given the same briefing documents (the input tokens), each analyst highlights different connections — one tracks pronoun references, another verb-object pairs, another positional proximity. Each produces a summary of the same length as the input. The final output is one synthesis from all specialist summaries, combined by the output projection. More heads means more analytical perspectives; but each specialist's report is shallower because the available representation budget (d_model) is divided equally.
